citations.narrative:
The facility has not received health certificates to accompany their dogs for transport in commerce since July 2025. The
facility continues to travel monthly for scheduled exhibition opportunities and events and has continued travel into the
2026 calendar. No health certificates have been issued to account for these itineraries associated with travel dates
occurring after July 2025.
Health certificates are important for ensuring that animals are healthy, suitable for travel, and free from signs of disease.
Exhibitors who transport dogs in commerce shall have a health certificate executed and issued by a licensed veterinarian
within 10 days of travel to accompany the animals.

One gasoline container was stored in the yard area sandwiched between the side of the facility and the outdoor exercise
enclosure housing twenty-one dogs. Two gasoline containers were present, but the applicant confirmed only container
had gasoline within it. While the gasoline container was stored within a smaller, fenced-in partition, dogs can make
contact with the container itself due to its placement beside the fence line where their noses can reach. Furthermore, one
dog was seen during this inspection entering the enclosed space where the gasoline and lawn equipment were stored,
due to a gap present.
Husbandry items inappropriately stored in or near the same areas housing dogs can be harmful or toxic if ingested by the
animal, or comes into contact with the animals. Substances that are toxic to the dogs but are required for normal
husbandry practices must not be stored in food storage and preparation areas, but may be stored in cabinets in the
animal areas.

An enclosure housing one dog did not meet the minimum amount of floor space as required by the Animal Welfare Act.
The primary enclosure housing Kymber provided 2.94 square feet of floor space, but this dog requires at least 5.44
square feet of floor space. Prior to measuring this enclosure, the licensee stated they knew the enclosure was too small
for this dog, but is preferred by the Kymber due to its location.
Failure to provide the dogs with the minimum amount of floor space does not allow for freedom of movement which could
cause discomfort and result in a negative impact on their health and well-being. Each dog housed in a primary enclosure
must be provided a minimum amount of floor space as required by the Animal Welfare Act. The applicant must provide
each dog with an enclosure that meets the minimum required floor space.

During this inspection, one dog was housed in an enclosure that did not meet the minimum interior height requirement of
6 inches higher than the head of the dog. The primary enclosure housing Kymber only allowed four inches between the
interior height of the enclosure and the top of the dog’s head while standing. While other primary enclosures were vacant
and available during this inspection that would meet the space requirements, the licensee stated Kymber prefers this
particular enclosure even though it’s smaller in size.
Failing to provide adequate head space in a primary enclosure prevents normal postural adjustments, including stretching
and extending its body to remain comfortable as it stands or rests. The interior height of a primary enclosure must be at
least 6 inches higher than the head of the tallest dog in the enclosure when it is in a normal standing position.

During this inspection, four adult dogs had empty water bowls within their respective primary enclosures. The licensee
explained all water receptacles had been filled prior to this inspection, refilling each water bowl again while APHIS officials
were present. Constant access to potable water is necessary to ensure that dogs remain hydrated as needed throughout
the day. The facility must ensure that all dogs have continuous access to potable water, unless restricted by the attending
veterinarian or excepted as provided in 3.17(a).
The facility corrected during this inspection.

The facility did not administer monthly heartworm preventative for July 2025. The Program of Veterinary Care (PVC)
outlines heartworm preventatives will be given on four week intervals (monthly basis), but none of the dogs received a
preventative during the July month. When asked why the monthly preventative was not given for this month, the applicant
could not recall the reason.
The written program of veterinary care affirms both entities (facility and attending veterinarian) have a clear understanding
of their respective roles to provide appropriate care for the dog housed on-site, as well as the outlines provisions detailed
to maintain the health of the animals. The facility shall ensure that the schedule approved by the attending veterinarian in
the PVC is followed by the facility for the sampling and treatment of parasites and other pests.

The dates for administration of various treatments for multiple dogs at the facility have not been recorded in full:
- Two adult dogs (“Teaser” and “K8”) should receive oral medication twice daily for chronic conditions. Upon review of
medical records, intermittent dates of administration ranging from September 2025 to February 2026 were recorded,
reflecting only one dose was given. When asked why the second dose wasn’t recorded, the applicant stated the dose
hadn’t been given on the days where only one ‘x’ was documented.
- Two adult dogs ("Grommit” and "Jester") received a water additive solution provided by the attending veterinarian
following their scheduled dental cleaning. While the applicant provided the solution to the water bowls for each dog
following the dental cleanings, the dates of administration were not recorded.
Adequate record keeping is necessary to ensure that the guidance of the attending veterinarian is being followed, and
helps the facility verify that medications were administered as directed. The facility must keep and maintain the names of
all vaccines and treatments and the dates of administration given to dogs at the facility.
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class “C” license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the
applicant.
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspections or by May 6, 2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection.
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
Additional Inspectors:
TANESHA WILKINS, SACS n

narrative:
2.78(a) 
   
Health certification and identification. 
The facility has not received health certificates to accompany their dogs for transport in commerce since July 2025. The 
facility continues to travel monthly for scheduled exhibition opportunities and events and has continued travel into the 
2026 calendar. No health certificates have been issued to account for these itineraries associated with travel dates 
occurring after July 2025. 
Health certificates are important for ensuring that animals are healthy, suitable for travel, and free from signs of disease. 
Exhibitors who transport dogs in commerce shall have a health certificate executed and issued by a licensed veterinarian 
within 10 days of travel to accompany the animals. 
3.1(e) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
One gasoline container was stored in the yard area sandwiched between the side of the facility and the outdoor exercise 
enclosure housing twenty-one dogs. Two gasoline containers were present, but the applicant confirmed only container 
had gasoline within it. While the gasoline container was stored within a smaller, fenced-in partition, dogs can make 
contact with the container itself due to its placement beside the fence line where their noses can reach. Furthermore, one

Husbandry items inappropriately stored in or near the same areas housing dogs can be harmful or toxic if ingested by the 
animal, or comes into contact with the animals. Substances that are toxic to the dogs but are required for normal 
husbandry practices must not be stored in food storage and preparation areas, but may be stored in cabinets in the 
animal areas. 
3.6(c)(1)(i) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
An enclosure housing one dog did not meet the minimum amount of floor space as required by the Animal Welfare Act. 
The primary enclosure housing Kymber provided 2.94 square feet of floor space, but this dog requires at least 5.44 
square feet of floor space. Prior to measuring this enclosure, the licensee stated they knew the enclosure was too small 
for this dog, but is preferred by the Kymber due to its location.

Failure to provide the dogs with the minimum amount of floor space does not allow for freedom of movement which could 
cause discomfort and result in a negative impact on their health and well-being. Each dog housed in a primary enclosure 
must be provided a minimum amount of floor space as required by the Animal Welfare Act. The applicant must provide 
each dog with an enclosure that meets the minimum required floor space. 
3.6(c)(1)(iii) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
During this inspection, one dog was housed in an enclosure that did not meet the minimum interior height requirement of 
6 inches higher than the head of the dog. The primary enclosure housing Kymber only allowed four inches between the 
interior height of the enclosure and the top of the dog’s head while standing. While other primary enclosures were vacant 
and available during this inspection that would meet the space requirements, the licensee stated Kymber prefers this 
particular enclosure even though it’s smaller in size.  
 
Failing to provide adequate head space in a primary enclosure prevents normal postural adjustments, including stretching 
and extending its body to remain comfortable as it stands or rests. The interior height of a primary enclosure must be at 
least 6 inches higher than the head of the tallest dog in the enclosure when it is in a normal standing position. 
3.10(a)  Repeat
   
Watering 
During this inspection, four adult dogs had empty water bowls within their respective primary enclosures. The licensee 
explained all water receptacles had been filled prior to this inspection, refilling each water bowl again while APHIS officials 
were present. Constant access to potable water is necessary to ensure that dogs remain hydrated as needed throughout 
the day. The facility must ensure that all dogs have continuous access to potable water, unless restricted by the attending 
veterinarian or excepted as provided in 3.17(a). 
 
The facility corrected during this inspection. 
3.13(a)(3) 
   
Veterinary care for dogs. 
The facility did not administer monthly heartworm preventative for July 2025. The Program of Veterinary Care (PVC) 
outlines heartworm preventatives will be given on four week intervals (monthly basis), but none of the dogs received a 
preventative during the July month. When asked why the monthly preventative was not given for this month, the applicant 
could not recall the reason. 
The written program of veterinary care affirms both entities (facility and attending veterinarian) have a clear understanding 
of their respective roles to provide appropriate care for the dog housed on-site, as well as the outlines provisions detailed 
to maintain the health of the animals. The facility shall ensure that the schedule approved by the attending veterinarian in 
the PVC is followed by the facility for the sampling and treatment of parasites and other pests. 
3.13(b)(3) 
   
Veterinary care for dogs.

The dates for administration of various treatments for multiple dogs at the facility have not been recorded in full: 
- Two adult dogs (“Teaser” and “K8”) should receive oral medication twice daily for chronic conditions. Upon review of 
medical records, intermittent dates of administration ranging from September 2025 to February 2026 were recorded, 
reflecting only one dose was given. When asked why the second dose wasn’t recorded, the applicant stated the dose

- Two adult dogs ("Grommit” and "Jester") received a water additive solution provided by the attending veterinarian 
following their scheduled dental cleaning. While the applicant provided the solution to the water bowls for each dog 
following the dental cleanings, the dates of administration were not recorded.  
 
Adequate record keeping is necessary to ensure that the guidance of the attending veterinarian is being followed, and 
helps the facility verify that medications were administered as directed. The facility must keep and maintain the names of 
all vaccines and treatments and the dates of administration given to dogs at the facility. 
 
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class “C” license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the 
applicant. 
 
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspections or by May 6, 2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee 
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection. 
 
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
TANESHA WILKINS, SACS n
